  file_changed stay true forever and nothing — not even re-editing the file — escapes. At
  minimum, conflict must have an exit. This is the bug-shaped part, independent of strategy.
  2. 3-way merge on reconcile (the real fix). When you hit db_changed && file_changed,
  recover the base body from plan_revisions and run a line-level diff3 of base / db_body /
  file_body. Clean merges (the common case) auto-resolve and go straight to synced. This
  leverages assets you already have; it's mostly wiring, not new storage.
  3. Conflict-copy fallback for true overlaps (Dropbox's trick). When diff3 does hit
  overlapping hunks, don't block — either write conflict markers (<<<<<<<) into the file and
  mark a lightweight needs_review, or spill the losing side to <name>.conflict-<ts>.md and
  surface it. Loss-proof, never sticky, and the human resolves it like a git conflict. This
  is where your "have a planning agent resolve" instinct actually belongs — not as the
  detector/merger (that should be deterministic diff3), but only as an optional convenience
  for resolving the residual overlap conflicts a human would otherwise handle.

  There's also a more radical option worth naming because it deletes the whole conflict class
  rather than handling it:

  4. Collapse the dual-write: make the file the source of truth, DB a rebuildable read-model.
  The conflict exists only because the same logical doc is authoritatively writable from two
  sides. If planning agents edited plans by writing the markdown file (same channel the
  human uses) and the DB were a derived projection you can rebuild from files, there'd be a
  single write path and no DB-vs-file divergence to reconcile — just last-writer-wins on one
  file, which is a normal editor problem. The cost is reworking how agents mutate plans.
  Given your memory note about "flexible substrate, not bespoke flows," this is the most
  architecturally honest direction, but it's the biggest change.

  My recommendation: 2 + 3 now (they reuse plan_revisions and last_materialized_hash, which
  already exist, and match git semantics your users already understand), with 1 as the
  immediate stop-the-bleeding, and 4 filed as the eventual simplification if/when plan
  editing gets reworked anyway.

  Want me to sketch the diff3 reconcile path concretely against sync.py — including how to
  pull the base body out of plan_revisions — so you can see how much actually changes?

✻ Waiting for 1 background agent to finish

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
❯ yeah, sketch the diff3 reconcile path against sync.py
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ⏵⏵ bypass permissions on (shift+tab to cycle) · ← for agents · ↓ to manage

  ● main                                                        ↑/↓ to select · Enter to view
  ◯ general-purpose  Reconcile conflicted plan                         1m 9s · ↓ 23.1k tokens
